schools.summary: Wesley: Samuel wisely delayed his rebuke until Saul's throne was secure and the people rejoicing, lest their fickleness make them cast off the king as rashly as they sought him.
Keil and Ellicott read a formal transfer of the judgeship to the king; Ellicott calls Samuel the link between judges and kings, presenting Saul as 'Anointed of the Eternal.'
Henry hears Samuel's apologia — old and gray-headed, having led the flock from childhood — using the people's joy as the door to their conscience.
Gill sets the address amid the people's mirth after Saul's establishment, the fitting moment to reprove their sin of desiring a king.
Ellicott reads 'walketh before you' as the shepherd going before his flock (cf. John 10:27); Keil parses the Hebrew verbs of the coming appeal as the strongest denial of injustice.
Wesley and Clarke: Samuel has fully resigned power and reduced his own sons to private citizens, now answerable to law like any other man.
Gill: the king now holds full regal power, so Samuel is above suspicion of clinging to office with any regret.

debates.question: What tone lies behind 'my sons are with you'?

sides.position: a public act of justice — the sons demoted to common citizens, open to prosecution
also a father's mortified feeling and quiet plea for their goodwill

schools.summary: Clarke: no minister ever resigned with such conscious integrity; the appeal is at once a magistrate's abandonment of his sons to justice and a father's quiet plea for them.

Ellicott: ox and ass are the representative possessions of a primitive age (Exodus 20:17), 'bribe' is literally ransom, and 'his anointed' is the earliest royal use of the title that points to Christ.
Gill and Wesley: the protestation is not ostentation but vindication, clearing Samuel so he may freely reprove the people's sin.
Keil: in acquitting Samuel so fully the people condemn themselves, acknowledging on oath there was no ground for their dissatisfaction and thus no reason for a king.
Clarke: of what governor of any nation could such spotless things ever be said?
Henry: the people owned Samuel's rule was neither oppressive nor expensive — like Nehemiah he required not the governor's bread and coveted no man's silver.
Gill: the people answer as one man, a unanimous verdict of Samuel's innocence.
Keil stresses that God is called witness 'against' the people, whose acquittal of Samuel condemns themselves; Ellicott records the assembly's solemn double shout, 'He is witness.'
Henry: a clear conscience and good report are our comfort under slights, as Demetrius had a good report of all men and of the truth itself (3 John 12).
Gill: the oath binds the people against ever hereafter reproaching Samuel with corruption or injustice.
Ellicott and Keil read 'even the Eternal, [the witness,] who made Moses and Aaron' — 'made' in a historical sense, their call onto the stage of history; the Exodus is God's great call of love.
Henry: Samuel instructs rather than upbraids, reasoning through Israel's history to engage their love; the minister's work is to convince the judgment and win the affections.
Gill: Samuel rehearses God's former mercies to aggravate the ingratitude of rejecting God as King.

Keil and Wesley: the 'righteous acts' are the tsedaqoth — benefits flowing from God's covenant fidelity, His saving deeds called righteous because faithful to His own word; Ellicott hears Samuel's painful pleading.
Henry: 'stand still' in reverence and attention; religion has reason on its side (Isaiah 1:18), and the minister persuades to win the will.
Clarke: 'I have arraigned myself before you; I now arraign you before God' — the acquitted judge convenes the trial of the people.
Gill: a summons to patient, reverent attention before the great rehearsal begins.
Keil: the Exodus and safe guidance into Canaan are the 'first proof' of God's righteous acts; Ellicott: the deeds of loving-kindness of the Eternal King, in order.

Clarke: God ever raised up deliverers when Israel's necessities required; Wesley explains how Moses and Aaron 'brought them in' though they never crossed Jordan — as the principal authors of the entrance.
Gill traces Jacob's seed from settlement to bondage to redemption, the cry answered by a saviour.
Keil and Ellicott identify Sisera, the Philistines and Moab's Eglon as prominent examples cited out of strict order; idolatry is rebellion against the invisible King punished by withdrawn protection.
Wesley: Samuel concedes the judges' era was full of affliction but retorts that Israel's own forgetting of God caused it — the fault lay not in the government but in transgressing its rules.
Gill: to forget the Lord is to neglect His worship and serve idols; such forgetting God 'sold' into the enemy's hand.

themes: the true disease is sin not government

schools.summary: Ellicott: the invisible King, full of pity, forgave and delivered as soon as the people confessed; Baal and Ashtaroth were Phoenician sun-and-moon deities, their plurality underscoring their emptiness.
Gill: bondage and cruel oppression at last wring from Israel the confession 'We have sinned.'

themes: the cycle of sin and deliverance

schools.summary: Ellicott judges 'Bedan' most likely a scribal slip for Barak (cf. Hebrews 11:32), records Kimchi's Ben-Dan/Samson, and defends Samuel's naming of himself as a fitting instance of God's deliverance.
Clarke surveys Barak, Samson, Jair; Wesley prefers Samson or Jair (cf. 1 Chronicles 7:17) and drives home that Israel dwelt safe, so a change of government was 'mere wantonness.'
Gill glosses Jerubbaal as Gideon, keeping to the plain identifications.

sides.position: Barak, a scribal slip for a similar name (supported by Hebrews 11:32)
leave the obscure name as it stands — its very obscurity proves the speech genuine

schools.summary: Ellicott: Nahash's Ammonite invasion of trans-Jordan, culminating in the siege of Jabesh-gilead, was the immediate occasion of the demand for a king.

Clarke fixes the sequence (elected at Mizpeh, confirmed at Gilgal) and notes this was not the first demand; Wesley calls the desire for a king, while God was their immediate King, 'a manifest reproach against God.'
Henry: they had a king of their own asking, to their shame, yet of God's making, to His glory; God did not cast them off even when they in effect cast Him off.
Gill: at the first sight of Nahash they sought a king instead of applying to the Lord for protection.

themes: Nahash and the demand for a king

schools.summary: Keil: the doubled 'behold' stresses that Jehovah fulfilled the desire though the request was an act of hostility; the people not only chose but demanded a king of their own self-will. Ellicott: God grants the petition of those who virtually rejected Him.
Gill: God chose the king, yet at the people's request, so the choice was in effect their own.
Wesley: chosen by lot yet by the people too; the Lord has 'yielded to your inordinate desire.'

themes: the gift that is a judgment

schools.summary: Ellicott (with Payne Smith) and Keil read the Hebrew as an unfinished, ejaculatory wish — 'O that ye would only fear the Lord!' — the blessed apodosis broken off in emotion (cf. Luke 19:42).
Wesley: 'to go after the Lord' here is not a duty but a privilege — God going before to lead and protect; Clarke: on condition they rebel no more, king and people are taken under mercy.
Henry: following God is its own wages, both precept and promise; the sincere are given grace to persevere.
Gill: all true service springs from the fear of the Lord.

themes: obedience as condition, God's leading as reward

schools.summary: Keil parses the comparative 'as it was against your fathers' and rejects the LXX 'your king'; Ellicott records Kimchi's 'fathers = kings' but keeps the plain comparison to the ancestors.
Wesley: a king gives no advantage against God's displeasure — 'if we will not let God rule us, yet he will judge us.'
Gill: sinning, king and people alike fall under the same hand that struck their fathers.

Henry: the harvest thunder is a true miracle, foretold and out of season; it turns against Israel the weapon that once routed the Philistines (1 Samuel 7:10) and shames the hope of a king who cannot thunder like God.
Keil stresses the emphatic 'even now, see this great thing'; Ellicott calls the demand for a king the crown of a long rebellion, breaking the glorious ideal of Israel.
Gill: to 'stand' is the fixed attention of the mind, not merely the posture of the body.
Clarke: 'this great thing' is this unusual occurrence, an evident sign.

themes: the folly of trusting a king

schools.summary: Gill, Clarke and Ellicott marshal Jerome and the Kimchis to show that rain never falls in the Judean wheat harvest, so the storm could only be miraculous; Keil states its purpose — to reveal the greatness of the sin in asking a king.
Wesley: the rain is a sermon that God is displeased and commands all things in heaven and earth, showing the folly of rejecting His government.
Gill notes the reapers at work under a clear sky — proof the heavens gave no natural warning of the storm.

Clarke: a totally unusual, evident miracle; the people dreaded God's majesty and Samuel's power with Him. Wesley: the sign shames the hope in an arm of flesh — no king could thunder like God or command such forces as prayer.
Henry: rejoicing in their king, they were taught to rejoice with trembling — God is greatly to be feared, and His prophets for His sake.
Gill: Samuel 'called' not by command but by prayer, as the Targum renders it, 'I will pray before the Lord.'

themes: fear of the Lord and His prophet
the arm of flesh versus the arm of God

schools.summary: Clarke: no sin to have a king, but sin to put a man in God's place; and their acknowledging the sin without putting it away is 'the general way of mankind.' Wesley notes their shame in saying 'thy God' rather than ours.
Henry: many who will not have Christ reign over them would gladly have him intercede for them to turn away God's wrath.
Keil: the miracle inspired a salutary terror that drove the people to beg the prophet's prayers.
Gill: the confession was the unanimous cry of the people in the midst of the tempest.

themes: seeking the intercessor while refusing the ruler

schools.summary: Ellicott: a precious evangelical truth — no sin too great to repent of; Samuel foresees the Lamb of God and joins the chorus of Isaiah, Hosea and Paul calling the guilty to press on.
Clarke reads the concessive 'although ye have done this wickedness, the past God will pass by if you obey'; Wesley: fear not 'with a desponding fear, as if no hope were left.'
Henry: the terrors were meant to frighten them to God, not from Him — transgression displeases but does not throw us out of covenant.
Gill: 'fear not' being destroyed by the tempest — the storm need not end in despair.

themes: no sin too great to repent
fear driving to God not from Him

schools.summary: Clarke calls idols 'tohu,' the waste and formless nothing of Genesis 1:2; Wesley gathers the biblical verdict (Deuteronomy 32:21; Jeremiah 2:5) that they are mere nothings with no power to help.

Ellicott judges the 'for' (Hebrew ki) a copyist's insertion absent from all the ancient versions, tightening the verse to a single prohibition; Keil: to turn from the Lord is to fall to idols that cannot deliver.
Gill: to turn aside from His worship is to embrace what can only fail; whatever is put in God's place proves vain.

themes: whatever we make a god deceives us

schools.summary: Keil roots the promise in the great name God had acquired among the nations by guiding Israel (Joshua 7:9); Ellicott finds here the doctrine of election, resting on God's inscrutable will.
Clarke: not for their sake but His own name's sake — God drew His reasons from Himself; Wesley: salvation is free mercy, not merit, so He will not forsake unless they thrust Him away.
Gill: to forsake His people would expose His name to blasphemy for want of power or faithfulness; His honour secures them.

themes: the freeness that steadies the promise

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott (with the Berleburg Bible) hold up Samuel as a glorious example to rulers, persevering in prayer despite ingratitude; Ellicott traces his passage from judge to the higher, untouched office of prophet who teaches the good and right way.
Clarke: Samuel would count himself a sinner to cease interceding; the good and right way has no crookedness and no evil in it.
Gill: Samuel freely forgave their offence against himself and promised every good office in his power.

themes: teaching the good and right way

schools.summary: Clarke catechizes each phrase — fear, serve in truth, with all the heart — bidding Israel act as a child toward a loving parent; Wesley: 'only,' else neither prayer nor counsel will avail.
Keil: the 'great things' to consider are chiefly God's gift of a king — the very concession counted among His mercies.
Gill: the fear commanded is filial, not servile — a reverential affection embracing all worship inward and outward.

themes: gratitude as the spring of obedience
even the king a mercy to consider

schools.summary: Clarke hears the Deuteronomic 'life and good, death and evil' set before Israel; never a people more warned or profiting less, now monuments of God's justice and forbearance — 'Reader, what art thou?'
Keil: the bare severity of the close balances the promise of verse 14 — obey and continue after the Lord, rebel and be consumed, king and people together.
Gill: 'still do wickedly' means a settled course of revolt, idolatry and despising the prophets, not a single lapse.

themes: life and good, death and evil
